bytes.title: C - Rust Ownership and C's Manual Memory Management
bytes.body: C developers might find Rust's ownership and borrowing system intriguing, especially when dealing with manual memory management. In C, memory management is explicit, with malloc() and free() functions. Rust's ownership model ensures memory safety by enforcing a strict ruleset. The borrow checker prevents data races and dangling pointers. A Rust value can be either owned or borrowed, with borrowing divided into immutable and mutable variants. Lifetimes are used to enforce the rules, preventing invalid references. This approach eliminates undefined behavior (UB), a common issue in C.

bytes.title: c pointers - Raw vs. Dangling
bytes.body: Raw pointers in c are declared with the asterisk symbol (*) and can point to any memory location. However, they don't perform any memory management and must be manually managed. Dangling pointers occur when the memory pointed to is deallocated. To avoid memory leaks, use dynamic memory allocation functions like `malloc()` and `free()` carefully. The `NULL` macro can be used to initialize raw pointers.

bytes.title: C FFI & Interop — Calling C from Rust/Zig via C ABI
bytes.body: To call C functions from Rust or Zig, you need to use the C Foreign Function Interface (FFI). The Rust `libc` crate and Zig's `foreign` module provide the necessary bindings. However, you must ensure the C functions are compiled with the correct C ABI (Application Binary Interface). The C ABI dictates the calling convention, function signature, and data layout. When generating bindings, you need to specify the C ABI in the compiler flags, such as `-mabi=sysv` for the System V ABI. This ensures the generated code matches the C ABI and can be called from Rust or Zig.

bytes.title: C: Zero-Cost Abstractions with __builtin_prefetch
bytes.body:
Zero-cost abstractions are functions that don't incur any performance overhead when inlined. C provides the `__builtin_prefetch` function for this purpose. It hints to the CPU about the memory access pattern, allowing the compiler to optimize accordingly. This can be particularly useful when dealing with large arrays or cache-inefficient data structures.

For example, suppose we have an array of integers and we're performing a loop that accesses each element sequentially.
